3.3 Price-action frameworks & theory claim: Are Elliott Wave and Wyckoff testable theories or flexible narrative languages? · side_a: Practitioners argue recurring crowd behavior, nested trend/correction, supply-demand phases, and volume-price relationships provide context, scenarios, and explicit invalidation points. · side_b: Elliott allows alternate counts and is acknowledged as highly subjective; Wyckoff explicitly uses broad guidelines and judgment, and independent out-of-sample validation of the full schematic is thin. · why: Individual components such as trend, breakout, and volume can be formalized and tested, but the full narrative gains degrees of freedom from labels and hindsight. It becomes evidence-based only after the analyst fixes objective rules before seeing outcomes.
3.3 Price-action frameworks & theory claim: Does the broader technical-analysis literature support reliable profits? · side_a: Park-Irwin report more positive than negative or mixed studies, while Lo-Mamaysky-Wang find several algorithmically defined patterns contain incremental information. · side_b: The same review says data snooping, ex-post rule choice, risk measurement, and transaction costs block a conclusive profitability verdict; information need not survive costs as tradable alpha. · why: The strongest synthesis is conditional: some price structure is statistically informative, but practical profit varies by market, era, costs, and research discipline, and many flexible visual theories remain under-specified.
3.4 Trading by holding period: day, swing & position claim: Can a retail individual realistically day trade profitably for a living? · side_a: Marketing/practitioner framing (course providers, finfluencers, ‘quit your job to trade’ content) claims almost anyone with ambition and little capital can day trade for a living — the exact promise the Brazil study set out to test. · side_b: Rigorous academic evidence says it is ‘virtually impossible’: 97% of persistent Brazilian day traders lost money, >80% of Taiwanese day traders lost in a typical half-year, and gross profits generally did not cover transaction costs; Korea’s KCMI found 60% of new investors lost. · why: The disagreement is largely marketing vs measurement. The practitioner claim survives on survivorship bias (visible winners, invisible losers) and studies that failed to separate one-time from regular traders; controlled, exchange-wide data consistently shows the overwhelming majority lose, and outcomes worsen with frequency and leverage. The genuine nuance both sides can share: a tiny (~1%) persistent, skilled minority does exist (Taiwan’s 62-bps/day persistence), so day trading is not pure luck — but skill is exceedingly rare and hard to identify in advance, so the base-rate expectation for a new retail trader is loss.
4.1 Factor investing claim: Why persistent factor premia can exist in competitive markets. · side_a: Risk-based explanations treat value, size, profitability, investment, or market loadings as compensation for bad-state or otherwise undiversifiable risk. · side_b: Behavioral and institutional explanations point to underreaction, overreaction, lottery preference, leverage limits, benchmark mandates, and slow arbitrage; publication-decay evidence suggests at least some premia reflected correctable mispricing. · why: The same return pattern can support multiple models, and statistical factors describe covariance more readily than causation. Investors should require a plausible mechanism but avoid presenting any one explanation as settled.
4.1 Factor investing claim: Whether the large published factor literature establishes many distinct investable premia. · side_a: Long histories, international replication, public data, and coherent economic stories support a small set of broad factor families. · side_b: Harvey, Liu, and Zhu’s multiple-testing critique and McLean-Pontiff’s out-of-sample and post-publication decay show that many discoveries are overstated, correlated variants, or competed away. · why: Evidence is strongest for parsimonious families replicated across definitions and regions, not for every named anomaly. Research significance must also survive trading, tax, capacity, and product constraints.
4.1 Factor investing claim: Whether smart beta should be called passive investing. · side_a: Once an index is fixed, the ETF follows published rules without discretionary security selection and is operationally passive. · side_b: Choosing a non-cap-weighted signal, universe, rebalance, constraints, and factor timing embeds active judgments and creates intentional tracking error versus the market portfolio. · why: Smart beta is best understood as passive implementation of an active exposure decision. The distinction matters because low turnover and transparency do not eliminate model risk or performance-chasing behavior.
4.1 Factor investing claim: Whether apparently distinct factor labels represent independent return sources. · side_a: Value, momentum, quality, size, and low volatility have different signals and can diversify one another; value and momentum have historically been negatively correlated in broad evidence. · side_b: Product definitions can produce large overlaps: dividend yield is partly value, quality can dominate a low-volatility or size screen, and sector or market-cap tilts can explain much of a labeled factor’s realized behavior. · why: Investors must measure actual holdings and regression loadings rather than count fund names or marketing labels. Multifactor diversification only works when exposures are real, controlled, and net of costs.
4.2 Systematic & algorithmic strategies claim: Does an impressive historical time-series-momentum result establish a durable trend premium? · side_a: Moskowitz, Ooi, and Pedersen found own-return persistence across 58 liquid futures and strong diversified historical performance. · side_b: Kim, Tse, and Wald found much of the alpha was associated with volatility scaling and that unscaled momentum was not significantly different from unscaled buy-and-hold. · why: The signal, leverage/volatility target, asset allocation, and benchmark are separate return sources. A faithful backtest must report each rather than label the whole portfolio ‘momentum alpha.’
4.2 Systematic & algorithmic strategies claim: Is a final holdout enough to validate a strategy? · side_a: An untouched chronological test period is essential because it prevents direct tuning on the reported final sample. · side_b: Bailey and coauthors caution that ordinary holdout can be unreliable for investment backtests, while a researcher can indirectly overfit through repeated strategy selection before the final test. · why: Time dependence, small samples, repeated trials, and researcher discretion require a wider evidence package: walk-forward tests, trial counts, robustness, multiple-testing controls, and shadow-live results.
4.2 Systematic & algorithmic strategies claim: Does mean reversion offer an easier retail edge than momentum? · side_a: Short-term reversal is empirically documented and can be understood as compensation for providing liquidity after temporary order imbalances. · side_b: The effect is strongest where liquidity is poor, and live-cost evidence finds short-term reversal much more constrained by trading costs than value or momentum. · why: The same friction that can create gross reversal returns also makes them difficult to capture. Bar-data backtests can turn non-executable price moves into fictional profits.
4.2 Systematic & algorithmic strategies claim: Can AI materially improve a retail systematic process? · side_a: AI can reduce research labor through summarization, information extraction, code scaffolding, tests, document parsing, and monitoring. · side_b: Regulators document hallucination, drift, bias, privacy, auditability, and agent-authority risks; cheap idea generation can also multiply hidden statistical trials. · why: AI is most defensible as a supervised productivity and control layer whose outputs are verified. Treating it as an autonomous market oracle shifts uncertainty from visible rules into opaque data and model failure.
5.1 Passive philosophy & the active/passive evidence claim: Are markets efficient enough that active management is pointless, so everyone should index? · side_a: EMH (Fama) plus the SPIVA/persistence/Barometer evidence say prices reflect available information and the average active manager cannot beat a cheap index after costs, and winners don’t persist — so passive indexing is the rational default. · side_b: The Grossman-Stiglitz paradox proves markets cannot be perfectly efficient — if everyone indexed, no one would gather information and prices would stop being informative — and Shiller-style evidence shows prices can be predictably wrong, so some active work is necessary for the very efficiency indexing relies on. · why: EMH and Grossman-Stiglitz are two levels of the same coin: markets are efficient BECAUSE active investors keep working, so ‘index because markets are efficient’ is self-limiting — it works for the individual precisely because not everyone does it. It is an equilibrium/composition argument, not a data disagreement.
5.1 Passive philosophy & the active/passive evidence claim: Does the rise of passive investing distort markets and pose systemic risk, or is the worry overblown? · side_a: Critics (Fraser-Jenkins’s ‘worse than Marxism,’ Michael Green’s inelastic-market/passive-bubble thesis, the ‘rise of mega-firms’ literature) argue index flows are price-insensitive, mechanically inflate the largest stocks, concentrate the index, reinforce momentum, and make markets more fragile to reversals. · side_b: Defenders note active managers still set marginal prices, passive is a far smaller share of trading VOLUME than of assets, and for any individual investor the cost/persistence arithmetic still overwhelmingly favors indexing — the systemic critique is about the aggregate, not a reason for a given investor to pay up for active. · why: This is a systemic-vs-individual framing gap (mirrored in node 1.4): the market-structure concern can be real while the individual’s best choice remains a low-cost index core, with concentration addressed at the portfolio level (nodes 5.2, 8.1) rather than by abandoning passive.
5.1 Passive philosophy & the active/passive evidence claim: Is a broad cap-weighted index fund genuinely diversified? · side_a: By construction it holds hundreds or thousands of stocks across the market, which is the textbook definition of diversification and why it is recommended as a one-fund core. · side_b: When the top 10 names exceed a third of the S&P 500 and only ~30% of stocks beat the index, a cap-weighted fund is effectively a concentrated momentum bet on a few mega-cap tech firms — diversified in name count but not in risk contribution. · why: The two sides measure diversification differently — number of holdings vs concentration of risk/weight. Both are literally true; the disagreement is about which metric matters, and it is resolved downstream (8.1) via equal-weight/ex-mega-cap/international tilts rather than by rejecting indexing.
5.2 Fund vehicles: index funds vs ETFs vs direct indexing claim: Is an ETF categorically better than an index mutual fund? · side_a: ETF structure offers intraday trading, portability, and in-kind redemptions that can reduce capital-gains distributions in taxable accounts. · side_b: A mutual fund avoids bid-ask spreads and premium/discount decisions, supports exact-dollar automation, and can be behaviorally superior; in tax-advantaged accounts the ETF tax edge may have no value. · why: The better wrapper depends on account tax status, platform, contribution method, trading discipline, and total cost; wrapper labels do not determine benchmark exposure or investor outcome.
5.2 Fund vehicles: index funds vs ETFs vs direct indexing claim: Does direct indexing reliably add after-tax alpha over an ETF? · side_a: Historical simulations show meaningful tax-loss-harvesting potential, including a 1.10% annual estimate under specified tax rates, and more stock-level loss opportunities than a single ETF position. · side_b: FINRA warns customization can produce markedly lower benchmark-relative returns and higher fees; benefits depend on tax rates, gains to offset, contributions, charitable giving, future liquidation, and wash-sale compliance. · why: The disagreement is mostly assumptions and investor circumstances, not mechanics. Tax harvesting is a personalized deferral strategy, so a generic headline percentage cannot establish net value for a specific investor.
5.2 Fund vehicles: index funds vs ETFs vs direct indexing claim: Does ETF-ification mean ETFs are now synonymous with passive investing? · side_a: The ETF wrapper became dominant through low-cost index products and still delivers the passive exposure discussed in node 5.1. · side_b: SEC and Morningstar data show rapid active-ETF growth; active ETFs have greater turnover, derivative use, and benchmark deviation, and nearly 1,000 launched in 2025. · why: ETF describes the trading and fund structure, not the portfolio-selection rule. Investors must read the strategy and benchmark before evaluating the wrapper.
5.2 Fund vehicles: index funds vs ETFs vs direct indexing claim: Is expense ratio the decisive fund-selection metric? · side_a: Expense ratio is certain, recurring, and directly reduces return, so it is a strong first screen among otherwise identical products. · side_b: Realized tracking difference, bid-ask spread, premium/discount behavior, tax distributions, platform fees, replication quality, and closure risk can exceed a tiny headline fee difference. · why: Expense ratio is one component of total ownership cost. Compare products on the same benchmark and measure both ongoing portfolio drag and entry/exit friction.
6.1 International & cross-border equity investing claim: How much of an equity portfolio should be international · side_a: Academic lifecycle research (Anarkulova, Cederburg, O’Doherty) argues the optimal split is heavily international — roughly one-third domestic / two-thirds international regardless of age, i.e. US investors are drastically underexposed abroad. · side_b: Practitioner guidance and observed behavior favor far less: Vanguard finds 30-40% international captures 95%+ of the diversification benefit, and US investors actually hold 80-85% domestic; a decade-plus of US outperformance has ‘rewarded’ home bias. · why: The academic case is forward-looking (diversification and mean-reverting real exchange rates over 100+ year global samples), while the practitioner/behavioral reality is anchored to realized US outperformance and to cost, tax, and currency-matching frictions that make some home bias defensible. Recency risk cuts against the backward-looking view.
6.1 International & cross-border equity investing claim: Whether to hedge currency risk on international equities · side_a: Hedging (via hedged share classes/forwards) removes FX volatility and is valuable for investors who want to reduce short-term swings or whose spending is entirely in home currency. · side_b: For long-horizon equity investors, Vanguard-style evidence says currency is roughly mean-neutral over time and hedging only adds cost, so equities are best left unhedged (hedging matters mainly for foreign bonds where currency dominates volatility). · why: The disagreement is about horizon and asset class: hedging clearly helps lower-volatility bonds and short horizons, but for equities the added cost outweighs a currency effect that historically washes out, so the mainstream long-term recommendation is unhedged equity exposure.
6.2 REITs & equity-adjacent income instruments claim: Whether a dedicated listed-equity-REIT allocation reliably diversifies a broad equity portfolio. · side_a: Property cash flows, lease structures and real-asset exposure differ from many operating companies, so a carefully selected REIT sleeve can reduce risk in a concentrated portfolio and add sector exposure that direct property would provide only illiquidly. · side_b: Listed REITs remain levered public equities, broad indexes already include them, and correlations rise or fall with market conditions, sector composition and the individual REIT; a separate allocation can simply create concentration. · why: Diversification is a portfolio-level, time-varying outcome, not a product label. The relevant test is marginal risk and return after considering existing property, financial, duration and equity exposures.
6.2 REITs & equity-adjacent income instruments claim: Whether REITs are inflation hedges or interest-rate-sensitive income stocks. · side_a: Replacement cost, contractual escalators and the ability to reset rents can let nominal property income rise with inflation, especially where demand is strong and leases are short. · side_b: Higher real rates can raise discount and capitalization rates, increase refinancing and acquisition costs, and compete with REIT yields; long leases, vacancies and operating costs can delay or overwhelm rent resets. · why: The result depends on why rates and inflation changed, lease duration and pricing power, sector supply-demand, leverage and debt maturity, and starting valuation. A one-variable rates rule is unreliable.
6.2 REITs & equity-adjacent income instruments claim: Whether FFO/AFFO is superior to GAAP earnings for REIT payout analysis. · side_a: GAAP historical-cost depreciation can make economically appreciating or long-lived real property appear to lose value mechanically, so FFO and maintenance-capex-adjusted AFFO better approximate recurring operations. · side_b: FFO can overstate distributable cash when upkeep and leasing costs are real, while AFFO is nonstandard and management can choose favorable adjustments. · why: Use both: reconcile GAAP net income to FFO, normalize recurring capex to AFFO, compare with cash flow, and challenge every add-back rather than treating any single metric as truth.
6.2 REITs & equity-adjacent income instruments claim: Whether preferred shares and BDCs can replace bonds in an income portfolio. · side_a: Preferreds offer contractual seniority to common equity and BDCs hold portfolios of interest-bearing loans; both may provide materially higher cash yield than investment-grade bonds. · side_b: Preferreds are subordinated, often perpetual and callable with deferrable dividends; BDC investors bear leveraged private-credit losses, appraisal uncertainty, equity volatility and fees. Neither promises principal repayment like a maturity-matched high-quality bond. · why: Higher yield pays for credit, duration, call, liquidity, valuation, leverage and governance risk. They may complement a bond sleeve but should be capitalized and stress-tested as distinct risk assets.
6.2 REITs & equity-adjacent income instruments claim: Whether MLP cash distributions indicate stable infrastructure-like income. · side_a: Pipelines and storage can earn fee-based cash flow under long contracts, and partnership pass-through structure can support substantial distributions. · side_b: Energy concentration, sponsor conflicts, customer credit, commodity-linked volumes, leverage and issuer-defined DCF matter; the SEC warns that borrowing or underinvestment can temporarily preserve distributions, and tax reporting can be complex. · why: Asset label and distribution history do not establish coverage. Investors must trace cash after maintenance capital, debt service, incentive rights and taxes through an adverse energy and refinancing cycle.
6.3 Options & derivative overlays for stock investors claim: Whether covered-call / buy-write strategies deliver superior risk-adjusted returns or simply truncate returns and underperform. · side_a: Early CBOE-commissioned research (Ibbotson 1988-2004; Callan through 2011) found the BXM matched the S&P 500’s return at ~two-thirds the volatility with clearly better risk-adjusted measures (Stutzer 0.22 vs 0.16; Sharpe 0.52 vs 0.30), suggesting the premium harvested more than paid for the capped upside. · side_b: From 2012 onward the relationship reversed hard (S&P ~162% vs BXM ~66%), and Morningstar concludes derivative-income strategies ‘are likely to underperform their underlying asset class over a full market cycle’ because the option seller was not adequately compensated for the forgone upside in a strong bull market. · why: The result is regime-dependent: covered calls trade the right tail for premium, so they win in flat/down/choppy markets and lose in sustained bull markets — and much of the favorable early evidence comes from CBOE/index-provider-sponsored studies over periods that happened to include large drawdowns, while the skeptical evidence spans the long post-GFC bull run.
6.3 Options & derivative overlays for stock investors claim: Whether high-distribution options-income ETFs are a genuinely good income solution or a yield illusion. · side_a: Issuers (e.g., JPMorgan) present derivative-income ETFs as a way to earn attractive, lower-volatility equity income, and the category’s ~$100B+ inflows and preserved-NAV examples (JEPI) show the payoff can be real and durable when distributions stay within what the strategy earns. · side_b: Morningstar and multiple analyses warn ‘not all yield is good’: high headline distributions (QYLD ~12%, some funds far more) are partly return of your own capital, NAV erodes, fees average ~0.52%, income is tax-inefficient, and single-stock versions can collapse (TSLY ~-70%). · why: The disagreement turns on distribution rate vs total return: a fund can pay a large ‘yield’ while its principal shrinks, so the honest metric is total return net of tax and fees — which for many of these funds trails simply holding the underlying, especially over long horizons.
6.4 Short selling & long/short claim: Does short selling ‘work’ — is it a source of edge? · side_a: Yes, as information/price-discovery: rigorous academic work (Boehmer-Jones-Zhang) shows heavily shorted stocks subsequently underperform and that ‘short sellers are important contributors to efficient stock prices,’ and activist short reports produce large, real price declines in targeted names. · side_b: No, as a standalone way to make money: the market’s long upward drift, daily borrow/dividend carry, and unlimited-loss/squeeze risk make dedicated shorting a chronic loser over full cycles — even Ackman (Herbalife, ~$750M) and the funds short GameStop (>$20B, Melvin’s $2.75B bailout) were crushed despite resources, and the activist edge decays within roughly a day of publication. · why: The two are not contradictory: the AGGREGATE short-seller population being informed does not mean an INDIVIDUAL short book is profitable, because being right on the thesis is necessary but not sufficient — timing, financing costs, sizing, and squeeze/recall risk determine whether the profit is ever collected. Shorting’s value is clearest as a hedge and a market-cleansing force, murkiest as personal alpha.
6.4 Short selling & long/short claim: Is short selling good or bad for markets — should it be restricted? · side_a: Regulators restrict/ban it to protect price stability and retail confidence: Korea banned shorting in 2020 and again 2023-2025 amid retail complaints of an ‘uneven playing field’ (기울어진 운동장) and rampant naked shorting, and the U.S. Rule 201 circuit breaker throttles shorting in already-falling stocks. · side_b: Academic evidence says short selling aids efficiency and punishes overvaluation/fraud, and studies generally find short-sale bans harm liquidity and price discovery without durably supporting prices; short sellers are among the few incentivized to expose frauds. · why: The disagreement mixes empirics with politics: the efficiency case is well-supported academically, but shorting is politically unpopular (it profits from others’ losses and is dominated by institutions), so authorities weigh perceived fairness and stability against price-discovery benefits — Korea’s compromise was not a permanent ban but heavy monitoring (NSDS), registration, and harsher penalties.
6.5 IPOs & new-issue investing claim: Whether IPO underpricing is evidence of an attractive opportunity for an individual investor. · side_a: Average offer-to-first-close returns are strongly positive, so an investor who receives a good allocation at the offer price can earn a large first-day gain. · side_b: Popular allocations are scarce and rationed, while most retail investors buy only after the opening premium; post-close long-run relative returns in Ritter’s 2001-2024 sample were negative on average. · why: The two investors pay different prices and face selection in allocations. Underpricing evidence cannot be transferred mechanically from underwriter clients to aftermarket buyers.
6.5 IPOs & new-issue investing claim: Whether waiting until after listing improves an IPO decision. · side_a: Waiting reveals trading demand, public-company reporting behavior, early earnings quality and insider selling, and can avoid a low-float opening spike. · side_b: Waiting can forfeit genuine underpricing and early appreciation when an exceptional company was conservatively priced and information arrives quickly into the market. · why: More information has option value, but so does earlier exposure. The choice depends on valuation at the actual purchase price, not a blanket rule to buy or avoid day one.
6.5 IPOs & new-issue investing claim: Whether lock-up expiry predicts a price decline. · side_a: Expiry makes previously unavailable shares saleable and can create a large supply shock, especially when insiders have strong liquidity needs and float was initially small. · side_b: The date and quantity are usually disclosed and may already be priced; insiders may not sell, good news may offset supply, and staggered releases reduce the cliff. · why: Lock-up schedules measure potential supply, not certain selling. They belong in scenario analysis rather than a deterministic trading rule.
6.5 IPOs & new-issue investing claim: Whether direct listings improve the investor proposition relative to traditional IPOs. · side_a: Direct listings can reduce conventional underwriting costs, avoid selective allocation and allow broad market price discovery in the opening auction. · side_b: They may provide less stabilization, no standard lock-up and fewer conventional underwriter functions, making the opening supply-demand balance and diligence more uncertain. · why: Removing an intermediary changes costs and access but does not remove adverse selection or valuation risk; the issuer, share mix and exchange mechanism still determine outcomes.
6.5 IPOs & new-issue investing claim: Whether SPACs are a faster, more flexible route to public markets or a structurally conflicted investment. · side_a: Trust cash, redemption rights and negotiated merger financing can offer an event-driven structure and an alternative route for a target to become public. · side_b: Sponsor promotes, warrants, fees, deadlines, projections, PIPE terms and high redemptions can create conflicts and severe dilution for post-merger holders. · why: Risk changes across the lifecycle: pre-deal SPAC units near trust value, merger-vote securities, and post-de-SPAC common stock are economically different positions.
7.1 Global macro & sector rotation claim: Does sector rotation actually beat buy-and-hold? · side_a: The cyclicality is real and rules-based sector-momentum backtests (e.g. ~13.94%/yr, 1928-2009, ~4%/yr over buy-and-hold) plus industry-momentum academic work suggest a capturable edge. · side_b: Discretionary macro timing has a below-median most-probable outcome and is indistinguishable from random (PMC), TAA funds have broadly failed to beat passive, and Fidelity’s own materials warn the strategy may underperform the index. · why: The disagreement is really about METHOD: systematic momentum rotation shows better (but not guaranteed, ~70%-of-years, deep-drawdown) evidence, whereas discretionary cycle-forecasting timing is where the failure evidence concentrates. Backtest results also depend heavily on period, costs, and survivorship.
7.1 Global macro & sector rotation claim: Is the yield curve still a reliable rotation trigger? · side_a: It inverted before every U.S. recession since the 1960s with a 6-24 month lead — a strong track record. · side_b: The prolonged 2022-2024 inversion without an immediate recession sparked debate over whether its predictive power had broken down or the lag had simply lengthened. · why: Even a historically reliable indicator has a variable and long lag, so acting on it mechanically can be early by a year or more or produce a false signal in a given regime.
7.2 Thematic, megatrend & ESG/sustainable investing Does ESG investing help or hurt returns? Friede, Busch & Bassen (2015) and some meta-analyses report a predominantly positive/non-negative ESG-financial-performance link, while many later meta-analyses (Revelli-Viviani, Kim, Atz et al.) find no significant difference and UC Berkeley Haas finds ESG scores add no information beyond financials. The disagreement stems from different samples, whether firm-level CFP or portfolio returns are measured, and whether financial controls (size/profitability) are applied — with which the ESG effect tends to disappear.
7.2 Thematic, megatrend & ESG/sustainable investing The exact ESG-rating cross-correlation figure varies: the Aggregate Confusion paper reports ~0.53 average across six raters while MIT Sloan’s write-up cites ~0.61, and decomposition shares differ (measurement ~50-56%, scope ~37-38%, weight ~6-13%). The divergence itself is robust; the point estimate depends on which rater set and normalization are used.
7.2 Thematic, megatrend & ESG/sustainable investing Has the ESG backlash peaked? Some 2025 data show continued US outflows and Europe’s first-ever annual sustainable-fund outflows, while other data show a Q2 2025 global rebound (+$4.9bn) led by Europe — sources disagree on whether the sector is in structural decline or a political-cycle trough.
7.3 Event-driven & special situations claim: Is merger arbitrage a low-beta absolute-return strategy or disguised catastrophe insurance? · side_a: Mitchell and Pulvino estimate positive excess returns after costs and little market correlation in flat or rising markets. · side_b: They also find correlation rises sharply in severe declines and the payoff resembles selling an uncovered index put. · why: Small, frequent closing gains can coexist with rare, clustered deal-break losses. Daily volatility and ordinary-market beta understate tail risk, so sizing must use break scenarios and crisis correlation.
7.3 Event-driven & special situations claim: Do spin-offs create a durable standalone anomaly? · side_a: Cusatis, Miles, and Woolridge report significant positive abnormal returns for spin-offs, parents, and combined packages. · side_b: Their 1965-1988 abnormal performance was limited to firms involved in takeover activity, and modern implementation, indexing, coverage, and transaction costs differ. · why: Separation can improve focus and expose hidden value, but the historical premium may partly reflect subsequent control transactions and sample selection rather than forced selling alone.
7.3 Event-driven & special situations claim: Does activist involvement create value or merely announce information and redistribute value? · side_a: Brav and coauthors find about a 7% announcement return, frequent success, and post-event operating and governance changes; Bebchuk, Brav, and Jiang find no long-run reversal. · side_b: Activists select targets, outcomes vary by objective, and event studies cannot fully separate selection, bargaining, transfers among stakeholders, and causal operating improvement. · why: A 13D is a catalyst and information event, not proof that the agenda is feasible or that buying after the announcement leaves an attractive risk-adjusted return.
7.3 Event-driven & special situations claim: Is bankrupt-company common equity cheap optionality or usually a value trap? · side_a: Negotiation, valuation uncertainty, and occasional priority deviations can leave some residual or option value for junior claims. · side_b: The SEC says old common is last in line and usually canceled, while NBER evidence shows distressed stocks have lower returns and much higher risk. · why: A nonzero chance of recovery does not imply a positive expected return. The correct object is enterprise value allocated through the legal waterfall, not the low nominal share price.
8.1 Asset allocation & diversification claim: Whether stock-bond diversification can be treated as a dependable crisis hedge. · side_a: The SEC, FINRA, and long historical experience support mixing assets that respond differently, and high-quality bonds have often cushioned equity losses. · side_b: The IMF finds that after 2019 inflationary shocks made stocks and bonds sell off together more often, weakening the classic 60/40 hedge during sharp stress. · why: Correlation is regime-dependent rather than a permanent asset characteristic. Growth shocks, inflation shocks, bond duration, credit quality, and starting yields change the hedge, so allocation should be stress-tested rather than built on one historical average.
8.1 Asset allocation & diversification claim: Whether today’s cap-weighted index concentration should cause investors to reduce equity exposure or replace cap weighting. · side_a: The S&P 500 top 10 are 36.4% and technology is 38.0%, so a nominally broad index has meaningful single-name and sector concentration; equal weighting sharply reduces that exposure. · side_b: Kritzman and Turkington confirm concentration but caution against acting solely to offset it, while Bessembinder shows aggregate wealth creation has always depended on a very small set of winners that broad cap-weighted ownership captures. · why: Concentration is a real exposure but not automatically a timing signal. The sound response depends on the investor’s risk budget: diversify globally and across assets first; use equal-weight or capped sleeves only while accepting their factor, turnover, tax, and tracking differences.
8.1 Asset allocation & diversification claim: Whether mean-variance optimization gives an objective target allocation. · side_a: Markowitz provides a coherent mathematical definition of efficient portfolios using expected returns, variance, and covariance. · side_b: The original paper acknowledges that probability beliefs and covariances must be estimated; in practice small input changes and regime shifts can generate radically different optimal weights. · why: The framework is robust as a language for trade-offs but fragile as a point estimator. Retail implementation is usually better served by constrained ranges, simple broad funds, and scenario tests than by an unconstrained optimizer.
8.2 Risk management & position sizing Do stop-losses improve returns? Andrew Lo & Kaminski’s theory shows that under a random walk with a positive risk premium a stop-loss ALWAYS reduces expected return (you sell an asset you should hold), yet their own and others’ empirical studies on momentum strategies show stops sharply cutting drawdowns and even raising returns. The disagreement is genuine but conditional: stops help where returns trend/auto-correlate and hurt where they are efficient/random — and tight stops plus transaction costs usually hurt regardless.
8.2 Risk management & position sizing Full vs fractional Kelly: Kelly is provably growth-optimal (maximizes long-run log wealth), which argues for betting the full fraction; but practitioners near-universally use half or quarter Kelly because full Kelly is extremely volatile and estimation error in the edge inputs makes over-betting catastrophic. One side prizes theoretical optimality, the other prizes robustness to being wrong about the inputs.
8.2 Risk management & position sizing Is VaR useful risk management or dangerous false comfort? Risk managers and regulators use VaR pervasively as a standard risk number, while Taleb calls it ‘charlatanism’ that ignores the tail and, by anchoring, actually encourages more risk-taking (echoed by Einhorn’s ‘airbag that works except in a crash’). The partial reconciliation is to use VaR only for everyday risk and supplement it with coherent, tail-aware measures like CVaR/Expected Shortfall plus explicit tail hedges.
8.3 Running the portfolio over time claim: Whether an available lump sum should be invested immediately or averaged into the market. · side_a: Immediate investment maximizes time exposed to the positive expected risk premium and beat three-month averaging in about 68% of Vanguard’s historical rolling one-year global-equity cases. · side_b: Averaging reduces the size of an immediate adverse outcome and can prevent a highly loss-averse investor from remaining in cash or abandoning the allocation after a poorly timed entry. · why: This is expected return versus behavioral implementability, not a forecast of the next market move. Prompt investment is the statistical default; a short fixed schedule is a behavioral accommodation whose opportunity cost should be explicit.
8.3 Running the portfolio over time claim: Whether rebalancing should happen on fixed dates or only after a drift threshold is breached. · side_a: Calendar rebalancing is simple, auditable, and easy for a household to maintain, especially with an annual review. · side_b: Threshold rebalancing responds to actual risk drift and can avoid unnecessary scheduled trades, but it requires reliable monitoring and a defensible band. · why: Both methods can restore target risk. The better operational rule is the one the investor can follow after taxes and costs; a hybrid can review on a calendar but trade only after a material breach.
8.3 Running the portfolio over time claim: Whether a falling stock price should trigger a mechanical stop-loss or a fundamental thesis review. · side_a: Mechanical exits can cap individual losses and may add value in return processes with sufficient momentum or positive serial correlation. · side_b: Under a random walk the expected stopping premium is negative, while a fixed price stop can crystallize normal volatility without learning whether business value changed. · why: A stop-loss is a strategy-specific risk overlay, not a universal law. Fundamental investors should make new facts, valuation, thesis falsifiers, and portfolio limits the primary triggers unless a price rule has been tested as part of the strategy.
8.3 Running the portfolio over time claim: Whether to let a successful active position run or trim it as its weight and valuation rise. · side_a: Rare long-term winners create disproportionate wealth, so selling solely because a gain is large can truncate compounding and express the disposition effect. · side_b: A rising position can breach the portfolio’s concentration budget or reach a valuation with inadequate forward return, making a tax-aware trim rational even if the business remains strong. · why: Past gain is not the decision variable. Compare current expected return, thesis strength, downside, correlation, position size, taxes, and alternatives under the prewritten mandate.
8.4 Performance measurement claim: Should time-weighted or money-weighted return be the headline performance number? · side_a: TWR removes investor-driven external cash flows and is the fairer measure of the manager, strategy, or asset-allocation process. · side_b: MWR reflects the timing and size of the investor’s actual contributions and withdrawals, so it is the economically relevant result for personal wealth and goal progress. · why: They answer different questions rather than competing for one correct label. A recurring-contribution investor should report both and never compare a personal MWR directly with a TWR index without cash-flow matching.
8.4 Performance measurement claim: Is Sharpe or Sortino the better risk-adjusted measure? · side_a: Sharpe is standardized, widely used, and summarizes differential return per unit of total variability under mean-variance assumptions. · side_b: Sortino avoids penalizing upside deviations and aligns risk with failure to reach a chosen minimum acceptable return. · why: Sortino introduces sensitivity to the target, downside definition, and sparse downside observations; Sharpe penalizes upside volatility and misses skew/tails. They are complementary diagnostics, not interchangeable verdicts.
8.4 Performance measurement claim: Does Brinson attribution reveal investment skill? · side_a: It reconciles active return to allocation, selection, and interaction and can show whether observed results are consistent with stated decisions. · side_b: The decomposition depends on benchmark, sectors, weights, data, period-linking, and interaction convention; a positive effect in a short sample can be exposure or luck. · why: Attribution is accounting for realized active return, not a statistical test of repeatable skill. Skill assessment needs persistence, risk, costs, factor controls, and out-of-sample evidence.
8.4 Performance measurement claim: Is the S&P 500 or KOSPI a sufficient benchmark for a diversified investor? · side_a: A broad headline index is transparent, familiar, and useful as a market reference. · side_b: Node 8.1’s diversified policy may include bonds, global equity, cash, currency policy, and active sleeves; a single-country equity price index mismatches those exposures and may omit dividends. · why: Use the headline index as a secondary reference, while the primary benchmark should be a pre-specified blended total-return policy index matching the investable mandate and reporting currency.
9.1 Behavioral biases Robustness of loss aversion: the strong universal 2.25 coefficient is criticized as fragile (estimated on 25 students; replication-crisis scrutiny), yet the broader prospect-theory framework replicated in a 19-country, 4,098-person study (94% of items). Both can be true — the marketable strong claim is oversimplified while the general reference-dependence/loss-aversion pattern holds.
9.1 Behavioral biases Does debiasing actually work? A 2024 experiment shows education significantly reduces the disposition effect (short-term), but the effect decays to non-significance by three months and the broader literature warns that simply giving a rational step-by-step procedure is ineffective. So debiasing is real but modest and temporary, not a cure.
9.1 Behavioral biases Is a bias always bad? Conventional theory says the disposition effect lowers returns, but the same 2024 study found that in mean-reverting markets a higher disposition effect was associated with higher returns — so mechanically removing a bias can occasionally backfire, complicating the ‘eliminate all biases’ framing.
9.2 Discipline, process & combining lenses How to resolve conflicting signals: the discretionary-integration camp (Schwab/Wealthsimple/newtrading) says reconcile by judgment — weigh by time horizon, pause and investigate on disagreement, and size to conviction — while the mechanization camp (enlightenedstocktrading) says do not reconcile at all; replace discretionary signal-watching with a single backtested rule set that ignores non-triggering indicators. Both aim to remove emotion but disagree on whether human judgment belongs in the loop.
9.2 Discipline, process & combining lenses Does combining lenses actually add value? It is near-universal practitioner advice, yet there is no rigorous evidence a hybrid outperforms a single lens, and purists argue mixing dilutes each method’s edge or invites curve-fitting. The ‘combine everything’ framing is intuitive but empirically unproven, and each constituent lens (value premium; technical patterns) is itself contested elsewhere in the map.
9.2 Discipline, process & combining lenses Rigid process vs. flexibility: a pre-committed IPS and rules protect against in-the-moment emotion (CFA), but blind rule-following can lock an investor into a broken thesis — echoing 9.1’s finding that mechanically removing a ‘bias’ can hurt in some regimes. The resolution is that durable process must build in structured thesis-review triggers (overlapping node 8.3), not just static rules.
9.3 Retail crowd & social dynamics What actually drove GameStop 2021? The SEC’s 2021 staff report found NO evidence of a gamma squeeze and concluded positive retail sentiment (not short-covering) sustained the rally; an ad hoc academic committee counters that a short squeeze mattered more and that the SEC ignored securities-lending data. Both agree social coordination lit the fuse, but disagree on which market-plumbing mechanism dominated — meaning the popular ‘short squeeze’ story is only partly settled.
9.3 Retail crowd & social dynamics Is social media net-harmful or net-democratizing for retail investors? The finfluencer evidence (56% antiskilled, negative selection, fraud-loss rates) is damning, yet regulators (FINRA) also credit social platforms with engaging previously underrepresented investors and lowering barriers, and a skilled 28% minority plus genuine idea-sourcing exist. Resolution: the platform is not uniformly bad; the average follower experience is bad because reach is decoupled from skill — so the answer is filtering, not abstaining.
9.3 Retail crowd & social dynamics ‘All finfluencers are scams’ vs the data: 28% are genuinely skilled (+2.6%/mo). The problem is not that skill is absent but that it is negatively correlated with influence, so the typical follower is steered toward the antiskilled majority. This complicates blanket dismissal while still justifying deep skepticism.
10.1 Idea generation, screening & research tooling claim: Whether a stock screener creates insight or merely compresses attention. · side_a: A hypothesis-led screen makes a huge universe manageable, enforces consistent definitions, and surfaces candidates a learner would not encounter through news or familiarity. · side_b: Lo and MacKinlay show that using observed data to construct and test sorting rules can produce severe data-snooping bias, while rigid thresholds can discard near-identical companies and hide data errors. · why: The screener is valuable as a reproducible research queue, not as evidence of future returns. Define the rule before evaluation, preserve rejected and near-threshold names, and validate with point-in-time primary data.
10.1 Idea generation, screening & research tooling claim: Whether machine-readable filing data can replace reading the document. · side_a: EDGAR APIs, standardized taxonomies, JSON and XBRL permit real-time, scalable cross-company extraction and comparison. · side_b: The SEC does not vouch for filing accuracy, companies can use custom tags, and economically important information often lives in footnotes, narrative context, amendments, and metric definitions. · why: Structured data is the index and calculation layer; the filing remains the source of context. High-impact values should be reconciled to the tagged fact, period, unit, footnote and latest amendment.
10.1 Idea generation, screening & research tooling claim: Whether earnings-call language provides decision-useful information beyond reported numbers. · side_a: Research finds Q&A tone has incremental explanatory power and that earnings-specific language outperforms generic dictionaries, supporting careful review of analyst-management dialogue. · side_b: Calls are voluntary, unstructured and managed communications; tone is sensitive to dictionaries, speakers, transcription, firm uncertainty and sample design, and does not establish an executable edge after costs. · why: Use the call to update questions, KPI definitions and management credibility, while treating the filed numbers and reconciliations as primary and avoiding mechanical sentiment trades.
10.1 Idea generation, screening & research tooling claim: Whether generative AI can be trusted as a financial research copilot. · side_a: A 2026 ACL system reached 79.7% human-verified precision on open-ended earnings-call KPI extraction, demonstrating material productivity potential for bounded tasks. · side_b: FinanceBench found an early retrieval-equipped configuration wrong or refusing 81% of tested questions, NIST treats confident falsehoods as a core risk, and a prominent disclosure-summary study was withdrawn after replication failure. · why: Performance depends on task, model, retrieval, document format, evaluation and time. AI should return traceable evidence and abstain when unsupported; human review and independent calculation remain mandatory.
10.2 Robo-advisors & automated/managed portfolios claim: Whether a robo-adviser is cheaper than self-directed indexing. · side_a: Automation can deliver a diversified portfolio, rebalancing, tax tools, reporting and sometimes human access at lower minimums and fees than a traditional full-service adviser. · side_b: The same underlying ETFs can often be held directly without an advisory fee; subscriptions are punitive on small balances, and product, cash, spread and tax costs remain on top. · why: The answer depends on the correct counterfactual and on whether the investor actually uses and values the additional services. Compare identical asset allocation and total annual dollars, not advertised headline fees.
10.2 Robo-advisors & automated/managed portfolios claim: Whether automated advice is genuinely personalized. · side_a: A digital system can consistently process goals, horizon, risk answers, cash flows and tax lots, then monitor and rebalance more frequently than many humans. · side_b: A short questionnaire can map many clients into a few models and omit labor income, housing, pensions, debt, outside accounts and changing family circumstances; SEC exams found limited-data and update problems. · why: Automation scales the variables it can observe. Personalization quality is determined by intake breadth, update process and household integration, not by the presence of an algorithm.
10.2 Robo-advisors & automated/managed portfolios claim: Whether target-date funds solve retirement allocation with one fund. · side_a: They diversify, rebalance and de-risk automatically, reduce choice overload and can serve as an effective default for investors who otherwise leave cash idle or chase returns. · side_b: The target year alone does not encode risk tolerance, pension wealth, savings adequacy, withdrawal timing or longevity; same-date glide paths and fees differ, and a ‘to’ or ‘through’ path can misfit sequence or inflation risk. · why: A TDF is a reasonable population default, not an individualized retirement plan. Its value is operational simplicity, while the investor still must validate the assumptions.
10.2 Robo-advisors & automated/managed portfolios claim: Whether tax-loss harvesting is a dependable robo-adviser benefit. · side_a: Systematic lot-level monitoring can realize losses promptly, maintain exposure with substitutes and defer tax more consistently than an inattentive investor. · side_b: Benefit depends on gains, tax rates, future realization, donations or death, substitute tracking and coordination across accounts; wash sales and turnover can erase or reverse value. · why: Harvesting changes the timing and character of tax rather than creating a guaranteed return. Evaluate realized after-tax household benefit net of fees, not the gross amount of losses harvested.
10.2 Robo-advisors & automated/managed portfolios claim: Whether passing Korea’s robo-adviser testbed demonstrates investment quality. · side_a: The testbed verifies minimum rationality, suitability, diversification, rebalancing, legal, security and operational controls and adds post-operation monitoring. · side_b: It cannot establish that future returns will beat a benchmark, that capital-market assumptions are correct, or that the service fits a particular household and fee/tax situation. · why: Operational validation and investment merit are different claims. Testbed status is a screening input, followed by product, allocation, cost and suitability diligence.
11.1 Execution & trading costs claim: Are passive limit orders superior to marketable orders for retail investors? · side_a: The recent nineteen-broker study finds lower costs and 60-65% fill rates for retail limits, with about a 20 bp advantage in smaller stocks. · side_b: Limits expose investors to non-execution, delay, monitoring burden, and adverse selection; urgent information-sensitive trades can lose more from waiting than they save in spread. · why: Order choice is an optimization over price, completion probability, and information decay, not a ranking with one universally dominant order type.
11.1 Execution & trading costs claim: Does payment for order flow harm retail execution? · side_a: Levy’s randomized trial says PFOF can benefit retail collectively through price improvement, though benefits differ by broker. · side_b: Broker agency conflicts are real, and simultaneous-order evidence finds large execution-price dispersion across brokers; PFOF revenue itself does not explain all dispersion. · why: PFOF funds zero commissions and can finance price improvement, but routing and wholesaler discrimination determine how much value reaches the customer. Broker-level execution statistics matter more than the PFOF label alone.
11.1 Execution & trading costs claim: Is VWAP an adequate measure of execution quality? · side_a: VWAP is simple, observable, and useful when the mandate is explicitly to participate near the day’s volume-weighted market price. · side_b: CFA guidance warns it often understates transaction cost because it misses the order’s market impact and does not begin at the investment decision; implementation shortfall is broader. · why: The right benchmark depends on the mandate. A favorable VWAP comparison can coexist with lost alpha during delay, while implementation shortfall can penalize a trader for market moves outside execution control.
11.1 Execution & trading costs claim: Did T+1 materially raise settlement risk? · side_a: A shorter processing window creates tighter funding, FX, allocation, and exception-management deadlines, especially for cross-border and manual workflows. · side_b: Observed U.S. implementation data show affirmation rose sharply and fail rates remained broadly in line with T+2. · why: Automation and preparation offset the mechanically shorter window at the system level, but individual investors still face an earlier cash-delivery deadline and market-specific cycle mismatches.
11.2 Tax mechanics How much is tax-loss harvesting actually worth? Industry sources market large numbers — Vanguard’s Advisor’s Alpha cites ‘up to 150 bps or more’ and its 2024 research 0.47%-1.27%/yr — while rigorous critics (Kitces, The Tax Adviser) net out the basis reduction and land at roughly 0.20%-0.30%/yr. The disagreement is real but reconcilable: the high figures count gross tax savings and assume reinvestment plus favorable future brackets, whereas the low figures recognize that harvesting mostly DEFERS tax (lowering basis) so the durable value is just the time-value of the deferral plus bracket/step-up arbitrage.
11.2 Tax mechanics Is tax-loss harvesting even relevant for a typical investor? Advisors and robo/direct-indexing platforms apply it near-universally, but The Tax Adviser notes that more than 90% of individual filers recognized no net capital gains in tax year 2020 and only about 5% had net short-term gains — meaning most people have little to offset, so the benefit that TLH marketing implies applies to a much smaller subset than the pitch suggests.
11.3 Korea account & tax selection claim: Whether ISA should be filled before taxable domestic investing. · side_a: ISA offers loss netting, a KRW 2/4 million exemption, low separate taxation above the exemption, and access before retirement after the three-year condition, making it a strong first wrapper for taxable interest, distributions, and domestic-listed foreign-asset funds. · side_b: Most small-shareholder capital gains on exchange-traded domestic shares are already exempt, ISA cannot hold direct foreign listings, and wrapper capacity, product expenses, tracking difference, and lock-up can reduce the advantage. · why: Prioritize scarce ISA room for income that would otherwise be taxable and for exposures available at acceptable cost; do not assume every domestic share benefits equally.
11.3 Korea account & tax selection claim: Whether pension savings or IRP should receive the next retirement contribution. · side_a: Both share the combined KRW 9 million credit base, and IRP provides the extra credit capacity after the KRW 6 million pension-savings base is used. · side_b: IRP has tighter withdrawal rules, prohibits direct stocks, and generally limits ordinary risk assets to 70%, while pension savings usually offers more equity-fund flexibility. · why: For a long-horizon equity investor, pension savings is usually the first retirement wrapper and IRP the second; liquidity and desired allocation can justify contributing less than the tax maximum.
11.3 Korea account & tax selection claim: Whether a domestic-listed or overseas-listed ETF is more tax efficient for foreign exposure. · side_a: A domestic-listed ETF can be used in ISA and pension accounts, may eliminate separate annual filing in ordinary low-financial-income or ISA-maturity cases, and integrates with Korean fund foreign-tax-credit handling. · side_b: A foreign-listed ETF offers a different fee, liquidity, product and capital-gains profile, uses the foreign-share KRW 2.5 million deduction, and may avoid Korean fund-distribution treatment, but requires foreign-share filing and cannot enter ISA or ordinary pension wrappers. · why: The winner depends on wrapper, turnover, distributions, foreign withholding, fund expense, tracking difference, realized gains, estate/treaty issues, and horizon; compare after-tax total cost, not ticker nationality.
11.3 Korea account & tax selection claim: Whether the 2025 foreign-tax-credit reform created permanent double taxation in tax-advantaged accounts. · side_a: Ending the advance refund removed a state subsidy where foreign tax exceeded Korea’s low wrapper tax, so some investors experienced a smaller visible tax advantage and operational confusion. · side_b: Current NTS guidance provides withholding adjustment for ISA maturity and tracks attributable foreign tax inside pension accounts for credit against Korean tax on qualifying withdrawals from 2026-07-01. · why: The reform changed timing and mechanics rather than imposing a single uniform result. Investors need the provider’s foreign-tax allocation data and must distinguish ordinary, ISA, and pension withdrawal cases.

Appendix B — Weak-Evidence Ledger (208 weak claims)
